front: What is Capacity Planning in IT Infrastructure?
back: The process of determining the future IT resource requirements (hardware, software, network, personnel) needed to meet current and projected business demands efficiently and cost-effectively.

front: Name the three types of capacity planning.
back:
1. Strategic Capacity Planning (long-term, 1-3 years)
2. Tactical Capacity Planning (medium-term, 6-18 months)
3. Operational Capacity Planning (short-term, daily/weekly)

front: What is the primary objective of capacity planning?
back: To ensure that IT resources are available in the right quantity, at the right time, and at the right cost to meet business requirements without over-provisioning or under-provisioning.

front: Define Service Capacity in IT context.
back: The maximum workload an IT service, system, or component can handle while meeting specified performance objectives and service level agreements.
The process of creating mathematical or simulation models to predict how systems will perform under various load conditions and to identify bottlenecks.

front: What is the role of SLAs in capacity planning?
back: SLAs define performance benchmarks (response time, throughput, availability) that capacity planning must ensure are met during peak and normal load conditions.
The measured and documented level of IT service performance under normal operating conditions, used as a reference for planning and comparison.
The rate at which a system processes data or completes transactions per unit of time (e.g., transactions per second, requests per minute).

front: What is Scalability in IT infrastructure?
back: The ability of a system to handle increased workload by adding resources (vertical scaling) or adding more nodes (horizontal scaling) without performance degradation.

front: What is the Difference Between Capacity and Performance?
back: Capacity refers to HOW MUCH work a system can handle; Performance refers to HOW WELL it handles work (speed, response time, efficiency).
Setting predefined limits for resource utilization (CPU >80%, Memory >75%); when exceeded, alerts trigger capacity review or scaling actions.

front: What is Load Balancing in capacity management?
back: A technique to distribute workloads across multiple resources to ensure no single component is overwhelmed, optimizing utilization and response times.
The percentage of available capacity currently in use, calculated as: Utilisation = (Current Load / Maximum Capacity) × 100%.

front: What is Right-sizing in capacity planning?
back: Aligning IT resources precisely with business needs—avoiding over-provisioning (wasted cost) and under-provisioning (service degradation).

front: What is a Capacity Plan Document?
back: A formal document outlining current capacity, forecasted requirements, recommended actions, timelines, and resource allocation for meeting future demands.
